turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: On this law day, 2017, Justice Breyer has two opinions for the Court.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: The first is in the case called Venezuela v. Helmerich & Payne International Drilling Company. Under the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act, foreign governments are -- tend to be immune for -- they are immune from lawsuits in American Courts unless there is an exception, the statute has quite a few exceptions and the exception we are dealing with is called the expropriation exception. Now, it says the Court does have jurisdiction even though with sovereign immunity they would not, but it does because it is an exception and plaintiffs can bring a lawsuit against a foreign government if among other things, “rights in property taken in violation of In'ternational Law are in issue.” Now, we have to decide how a court should apply that exception in the jurisdictional case, what is the standard for applying it? Now, in this case, an American company and its Venezuelan subsidiary supplied oil rigs to agencies associated with the Venezuelan Government. The companies filed suit in federal court because they said that Venezuela unlawfully nationalized the oil rigs. Venezuela moved to dismiss saying they had sovereign immunity, they were sovereign. The company's reply, “Oh, but we have rights in property taken in violation of International Law” so the exception applies. The District of Columbia Circuit ultimately held the expropriation exception does allow the suit to go forward. They did involve rights in property, taken in violation of International law, but because the companies had made a non-frivolous argument to that, that that was so. Now, you see the issue. Was that right to just say non-frivolous argument or should the D.C. Circuit really have just decided it? Were they the right standard or do you have to get to the -- just say did they or didn't they, not whether they made a non-frivolous argument to that effect. We conclude the circuit standard is incorrect. A case falls within the scope of the expropriation exception if the relevant factual allegations or the facts actually make out a legally valid claim th'at a certain kind of right is at issue, mainly a property right that the -- and that the property was taken in a certain way in violation of International Law. Those are the kind of rights that have to be at issue, and whether the rights that the Plaintiff puts at issue in the case are or not of that kind must be determined before a court denies sovereign immunity and exercises jurisdiction, a non-frivolous argument to that effect is not enough. Now as that, in other words, it is about the kind of rights? Whether the claimant has those rights or not, if they're the right kind can be a matter for the merits. Our reading is consistent, but the text with the purposes of the act, the expropriation exemption requires for example that a taking be in violation of International Law. If you read it, so did it applied where there is a non-frivolous argument, that argument might be not frivolous but it's raw. And if that is so, then you're going to get the sovereign coming in to court where there wasn't a violation of International Law. And that would embroil foreign sovereigns in American lawsuits by substituting for a workable standard, a standard that would be limited only by the balance of a lawyer's imagination, quite large and doing so could create friction with other nations and reciprocal actions against this country. Now, for these reasons and others that we set out at some length in our opinion, we vacate the judgment of the D.C. Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with our opinion. The opinion is unanimous. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
